east-rockaway-bay-park-microgrid.summary: Nassau County's 2016 NY Prize feasibility study evaluated a community microgrid centered on the Bay Park Sewage Treatment Plant and nine other critical facilities in East Rockaway, New York. It compared an advanced-digestion/biogas CHP core with wind, solar, fuel-cell, and legacy tri-fuel generation options; the report did not document a selected construction design. No later NY Prize construction award or commissioned community microgrid was found, and a later Bay Park electrical-upgrade project says the plant's four old 3.6 MW engine-generators were decommissioned after new utility feeders were installed.
east-rockaway-bay-park-microgrid.status: Feasibility study completed in 2016; no construction or commissioning verified through July 2026. East Rockaway does not appear on NYSERDA's published Stage 2 award list, and later Bay Park work replaced the legacy on-site generation role with upgraded utility connections, so the public evidence supports a stopped-after-feasibility outcome rather than an operating microgrid.

organizations.name: Nassau County
organizations.role: NY Prize applicant, feasibility-study sponsor, proposed microgrid owner, and owner of the Bay Park wastewater plant

organizations.name: Village of East Rockaway
organizations.role: Proposed principal community customer and owner of several targeted critical facilities

organizations.name: United Water Long Island (later SUEZ)
organizations.role: Wastewater-plant operator and project partner involved in evaluating generation and treatment-process integration

organizations.name: PSEG Long Island
organizations.role: Electric utility and study partner that supplied system and load information

organizations.name: Long Island Power Authority
organizations.role: Public power authority named as a Stage 1 project partner

organizations.name: National Grid
organizations.role: Natural-gas utility named as a Stage 1 project partner

organizations.name: New York State Energy Research and Development Authority
organizations.role: NY Prize program administrator and Stage 1 feasibility-study funder

organizations.name: H2M architects + engineers
organizations.role: Engineer for the later Bay Park Phase E4 utility-connection work that documented decommissioning of the plant's old engine-generators

specifications.label: Proposed advanced-digestion CHP
specifications.value: 2.5 MW reciprocating-engine CHP fueled by digester gas; modeled for continuous operation and 21,900 MWh/year

specifications.label: Other evaluated new resources
specifications.value: 1 MW wind, 1.5 MW solar, and 1 MW fuel cell using natural gas and/or digester gas; these were alternatives or optional phases, not a documented final build package

specifications.label: Legacy generation represented in the study
specifications.value: 5.5 MW of available tri-fuel engine capacity in the resource table, with digester-gas production limiting sustained biogas output to about 2.5 MW

specifications.label: Targeted facilities
specifications.value: Bay Park Sewage Treatment Plant plus East Rockaway Village Hall, three public schools, two fire facilities, the public library, public works, and the post office

specifications.label: Bay Park study load
specifications.value: The feasibility report listed the wastewater plant at 7.5 MW and 65.7 GWh/year, far larger than the other proposed community loads

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: No electrical energy-storage system was specified in the reviewed feasibility design

specifications.label: Feasibility benefit-cost ratio
specifications.value: 2.2 in the NY Prize screening analysis

equipment.detail: The study evaluated the plant's aging tri-fuel engine-generators as an interim islanding resource. A later H2M project page describes four 3,600 kW units at the site and says they were decommissioned; manufacturer and model were not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
A 2.5 MW reciprocating engine was preferred over microturbines for the digester-gas CHP concept, but no supplier or model was selected in the public report.

technicalDetails.detail: The concept placed generation inside the wastewater-plant fence and proposed a new 5 kV community distribution system outside it. Islanded operation was designed not to depend on utility communications, and the preferred generation path was to be black-start capable.
The report's capacity table combines existing resources and mutually exclusive or staged options. Its figures should not be added together as though every resource was contracted for one final plant.

The proposal served public and institutional critical facilities rather than residential customers. Nassau County would own the central source, the Village would be the main customer, and the U.S. Postal Service site would have required a separate participation agreement.
The project received Stage 1 feasibility funding but is absent from the complete published Stage 2 award list. That absence is evidence of no NY Prize Stage 2 award, not a formal cancellation notice.

H2M's later Bay Park Phase E4 work reports a present peak load of about 6 MW, new utility-feeder connections, and decommissioning of four old 3.6 MW engine-generators. The reviewed page does not describe the 2016 community microgrid as having been built.
No commissioning record, as-built one-line, equipment award, operating data, or explicit project-cancellation notice was found through July 2026.

edmondson-village-milligrid.summary: Baltimore Gas and Electric proposed the Edmonson Village public-purpose microgrid in its 2015 Maryland pilot filing. The design was a roughly 3 MW natural-gas system serving a cluster of critical commercial and public loads in Baltimore City. Maryland's Public Service Commission denied BGE's two-site proposal without prejudice in July 2016, so this was never a commissioned microgrid. Later literature's label 'milligrid' describes the proposed multi-node topology, not a built demonstration.
edmondson-village-milligrid.status: Proposal denied by the Maryland Public Service Commission on July 19, 2016; no construction or operation. The denial was without prejudice, but no revived Edmonson/Edmondson Village project was found through July 2026.

organizations.name: Baltimore Gas and Electric Company
organizations.role: Applicant and proposed owner, builder, and operator of the public-purpose microgrid

organizations.name: Maryland Public Service Commission
organizations.role: State regulator that reviewed and denied BGE's pilot proposal in Case No. 9416

organizations.name: PJM Interconnection
organizations.role: Potential wholesale-market venue for energy, capacity, ancillary-service, and demand-response revenues contemplated by BGE; no participation occurred because the project was not approved

organizations.name: Edmonson Village critical-service businesses and public facilities
organizations.role: Proposed resilience beneficiaries, including grocery, pharmacy, clinic, fuel, public-building, and emergency-shelter functions

specifications.label: Proposed generation
specifications.value: Approximately 3 MW of natural-gas generation for the Edmonson Village site

specifications.label: Proposed project cost
specifications.value: Approximately $9.2 million for Edmonson Village

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Nearly all natural gas, with diesel described only as a fallback if gas were unavailable; the filed design included no renewable generation, CHP, or energy storage

specifications.label: Proposed residential surcharge
specifications.value: $0.04/month for an average BGE residential customer in year one, increasing to $0.13/month in year two and continuing for recovery of capital, operations, fuel, and tax costs

specifications.label: Proposed market revenue
specifications.value: BGE estimated about $215,000 per year per pilot from possible PJM market participation, but deliverability and value were not established

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: None proposed

equipment.detail: Natural-gas generator technology was specified at the project level, but the public regulatory record reviewed here does not identify a manufacturer or model because the project did not reach procurement.

technicalDetails.detail: BGE proposed a utility-owned system that would separate from the distribution grid during a significant or extended outage and energize a cluster of critical community services.

The Commission denied the filing as submitted because BGE had not sufficiently established public benefit or site selection, the design relied on a single fuel and lacked diverse distributed resources, costs would be socialized while benefits were geographically concentrated, third-party competition was limited, and projected PJM value was uncertain.

The official order uses 'Edmonson Village,' while the Baltimore neighborhood is commonly written 'Edmondson Village.' A later Asian Development Bank handbook calls the proposal a 'milligrid' because the planned resources and customers sat at multiple points on BGE's grid rather than behind a single point of common coupling.

The reviewed filing describes a community cluster around Edmonson Village, not an operating project at an 'Edmonson High School.' The authoritative regulator record also supersedes later shorthand that may imply the design was tested in 2016: it was denied before construction.
Because the project stopped at the regulatory proposal stage, there are no as-built equipment models, commissioning tests, outage results, or operating energy data.

sources.title: Order No. 87669, Case No. 9416: BGE Microgrid Order
The Value of Resilience for Distributed Energy Resources: An Overview of Current Analytical Practices
BGE Proposes Testing Microgrids at Two Maryland Locations
Handbook on Microgrids for Power Quality and Connectivity

el-hierro-microgrid.summary: Gorona del Viento is El Hierro's operating wind-pumped-hydro power plant, coupling five Enercon wind turbines with pumping, two reservoirs, four Pelton hydro units, and the island's Llanos Blancos thermal station. It can run the isolated island grid entirely from renewable power for sustained periods, but it has not eliminated diesel annually: Gorona's 2025 report attributes 56% of island supply to the hydro-wind system and 44% to thermal generation.
el-hierro-microgrid.status: Operational. The official operator publishes production through 2025. A proposed 5 MW solar-plus-5 MW battery addition was announced in 2023, but no reviewed source confirmed it as constructed or commissioned by July 2026.

organizations.name: Gorona del Viento El Hierro, S.A.
organizations.role: Owner and operator of the wind-pumped-hydro plant

organizations.name: Cabildo de El Hierro
organizations.role: Majority shareholder; Endesa reported a 65.82% interest in 2023

organizations.name: Endesa
organizations.role: 23.21% shareholder reported in 2023 and operator of the Llanos Blancos thermal generation that supports the island system

organizations.name: Instituto Tecnológico de Canarias
organizations.role: Technical partner and 7.74% shareholder reported in 2023

organizations.name: Government of the Canary Islands
organizations.role: 3.23% shareholder reported in 2023

organizations.name: Red Eléctrica de España
organizations.role: System operator for El Hierro's isolated electric system

organizations.name: Enercon
organizations.role: Supplier of the five E-70 wind turbines

organizations.name: ANDRITZ Hydro
organizations.role: Supplier of the hydro plant's electromechanical package, including horizontal Pelton turbine-generator units

organizations.name: Elecnor
organizations.role: Main hydraulic-works contractor identified by ANDRITZ

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Supplier of a power-management system described in ABB's island-grid presentation

specifications.label: Wind plant
specifications.value: 11.5 MW total: five Enercon E-70 turbines rated 2.3 MW each

specifications.label: Hydroelectric generation
specifications.value: 11.32 MW final operating rating: four horizontal Pelton units at 2.83 MW each

specifications.label: Pumping plant
specifications.value: 6 MW: two 1.5 MW variable-speed pumps and six 500 kW fixed-speed pumps

specifications.label: Reservoirs and head
specifications.value: Upper reservoir 380,000 m³ at about 700 m elevation; lower reservoir 150,000 m³ at about 50 m; gross hydraulic head about 655 m

specifications.label: 2025 island energy split
specifications.value: 56% Gorona hydro-wind and 44% Llanos Blancos thermal generation

specifications.label: 2025 wind production
specifications.value: 30,282.498 MWh gross and 29,922.394 MWh net

specifications.label: 2025 hydro production and pumping
specifications.value: 6,235.930 MWh gross hydro, 5,588.977 MWh net hydro, and 13,185.600 MWh consumed by pumping

specifications.label: Announced future addition
specifications.value: A first phase of 5 MW photovoltaic generation and a 5 MW battery at Frontera was announced in 2023 as a plan, not an as-built asset

equipment.detail: Five direct-drive wind turbines, 2.3 MW each

Four horizontal Pelton units. ANDRITZ's supply article used a 2.86 MW/unit contract figure, while the operator's final system page lists 2.83 MW/unit and 11.32 MW total; both dated contexts are retained.
ABB presentation material identifies an ABB power-management system coordinating the islanded hybrid plant; the reviewed public document does not provide a product model.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind power can supply the grid directly or drive pumps that move water to the upper reservoir. Stored water is released through Pelton turbines when wind is insufficient; speed governors and flywheels support frequency control while the thermal station remains available for balancing and adequacy.
Red Eléctrica reported 18 consecutive days of 100% renewable supply from January 25 to February 12, 2018. In 2017, renewables supplied 20,234 MWh, or 46.5% of annual demand.

IDAE describes a €79 million project and Endesa dates startup to 2015. Public sources sometimes use 2014 for inauguration and 2015 for regular startup, so 2015 is used here for operating service rather than claiming one universal commissioning date.

Claims that El Hierro is a '100% renewable island' describe the design objective and periods of full renewable operation, not the annual mix. The operator's 2025 accounting still shows 44% thermal supply, and pumping consumption must not be counted as net generation.

No reviewed source confirmed construction, battery vendor, storage energy duration, or commissioning for the 2023 photovoltaic-and-battery proposal by July 2026. Current unit-level details for Llanos Blancos diesel generation were also not established in this review.

experimental-power-grid-centre.summary: Singapore's Experimental Power Grid Centre opened on Jurong Island in 2011 as a configurable 1 MW research microgrid where generation, storage, loads, and grid disturbances could be reproduced without risking the public network. The original A*STAR/ICES facility included diesel generators, solar, multiple storage chemistries, emulators, and Meidensha's MicroGrid Central Controller. It now operates under Nanyang Technological University's Energy Research Institute as the Electrification and Power Grids Centre, with updated storage, power-electronics, motor, simulation, and hardware-in-the-loop platforms.
experimental-power-grid-centre.status: Operational under Nanyang Technological University as the Electrification and Power Grids Centre. The site remains active in 2026 and is hosting a new FutureGrid Accelerator testbed; public sources do not establish which original 2011 assets remain in service unchanged.

organizations.name: Agency for Science, Technology and Research
organizations.role: Original owner and launcher of the Experimental Power Grid Centre

organizations.name: Institute of Chemical and Engineering Sciences
organizations.role: A*STAR institute that originally operated the Jurong Island research facility

organizations.name: Nanyang Technological University
organizations.role: Current institutional home of the renamed Electrification and Power Grids Centre

organizations.name: Energy Research Institute @ NTU
organizations.role: Current operator and research organization for EPGC

organizations.name: Meidensha Corporation
organizations.role: Turnkey system supplier for the original building, MicroGrid Central Controller, distributed resources, emulators, and test infrastructure

organizations.name: Rolls-Royce, Vestas, and SP PowerGrid
organizations.role: Industrial research collaborators named around the original launch program

organizations.name: ST Telemedia Global Data Centres
organizations.role: Partner with NTU on the 2026 FutureGrid Accelerator high-voltage direct-current and AI-infrastructure testbed hosted at EPGC

specifications.label: Original research-grid rating
specifications.value: 1 MW configurable experimental power grid

specifications.label: Original diesel generation
specifications.value: Three generator sets rated 40 kW, 100 kW, and 200 kW (340 kW total)

specifications.label: Original photovoltaic system
specifications.value: 60 kW

specifications.label: Original electrochemical storage
specifications.value: 110 kWh lead-acid plus two 20 kWh lithium-ion systems (40 kWh lithium-ion total); public paper does not provide comparable power ratings for all batteries

specifications.label: Original ultracapacitors
specifications.value: 60 kW and 90 kW units, each specified for approximately ±30 seconds

specifications.label: Original emulators and loads
specifications.value: 90 kW four-quadrant grid emulator, 10 kW wind emulator, and programmable loads of 50 kW, 150 kW, and 300 kW

specifications.label: Original electrical bus
specifications.value: 415 V, three-phase four-wire, 50 Hz, 3,000 A test bus interfaced to Singapore's 22 kV network

specifications.label: Current listed test platforms
specifications.value: Includes a 200 kW energy-storage test system, 500 kW motor platform, 100 kW PV-inverter platform, real-time simulation and hardware-in-the-loop, multi-functional electrical testing, and building-energy-management research

equipment.detail: Original supervisory controller for dispatch, test sequencing, and grid-connected/islanded experiments

Lead-acid, lithium-ion, and two short-duration ultracapacitor systems allowed comparison of energy- and power-oriented storage. Component manufacturers beyond the turnkey integrator were not disclosed in the reviewed paper.

NTU lists energy-storage, large-motor, PV-inverter, controller-hardware-in-the-loop, and real-time digital simulation facilities; its page does not equate these current platforms one-for-one with the original 2011 equipment.

technicalDetails.detail: Three buses could be operated independently or rearranged in series, parallel, and loop configurations. The emulators let researchers impose voltage, frequency, renewable-output, and load conditions without disturbing the public power system.

The facility officially opened on November 1, 2011. A*STAR described continuing industrial microgrid work in 2018, and the International Energy Agency still listed the 1 MW center as an in-force measure in its June 2025 update.

The acronym EPGC is retained, but the current name is Electrification and Power Grids Centre rather than Experimental Power Grid Centre. It is now presented as an NTU Energy Research Institute capability rather than an A*STAR/ICES facility.

NTU announced the FutureGrid Accelerator at EPGC in 2026 to study high-voltage direct-current architectures, AI/data-center power demand, and next-generation infrastructure with ST Telemedia Global Data Centres.
A*STAR's 2018 article also describes a much larger Sabang Island project. Its 1.4 MWp solar, 1.2 MW diesel, and 2.3 MWh battery ratings belong to Sabang, not to the Jurong Island EPGC test grid.
Current public pages do not give a retirement/retention inventory for the original diesel sets, PV array, batteries, ultracapacitors, or MGCC, so their 2026 service status is explicitly unknown.

f-grid-project-toyota-plant-tohoku-area-.summary: F-Grid Miyagi-Ohira is a factory-centered smart-community energy system in the Second North Sendai Central Industrial Area at Ohira Village, Miyagi Prefecture. Since 2013 it has combined utility purchases, a 7.8 MW city-gas cogeneration plant, solar, reused vehicle batteries, private distribution, heat delivery, and a community energy-management system across participating factories, with an emergency pathway to the Ohira Village disaster-management office.
f-grid-project-toyota-plant-tohoku-area-.status: Operating program. Toyota still publishes a current F-Grid LLP business sheet and profiled continuing F-Grid leadership activity in December 2024. No recent annual generation, fuel-use, emissions, outage, or availability statistics were found through July 2026.

organizations.name: F-Grid Miyagi-Ohira Limited Liability Partnership
organizations.role: Owner/operator entity that supplies electricity and heat, manages shared energy assets, and provides emergency power

organizations.name: Yokogawa Solution Service Corporation
organizations.role: Developer, integrator, and supplier of the community energy-management system that became fully operational in 2015

organizations.name: Ohira Village
organizations.role: Neighboring public beneficiary; the village office/disaster-management center can receive emergency electricity from F-Grid

specifications.label: Current solar rating
specifications.value: 740 kW in Toyota's current business sheet; early operating material reported about 700 kW, indicating a dated configuration difference

specifications.label: Reused-battery power
specifications.value: 90 kW in Toyota's current business sheet; the original launch configuration was 50 kW split between 30 kW and 20 kW reused Prius-battery systems. Energy capacity and duration were not published.

specifications.label: Emergency community supply
specifications.value: Up to 1.5 MW can be sent to the Ohira Village office/disaster-management center through the emergency arrangement described at launch

specifications.label: Mobile emergency resources
specifications.value: Original design included eight plug-in hybrid vehicles, each able to provide 100 V / 1.5 kW auxiliary output

specifications.label: Capital and membership
specifications.value: Toyota's current sheet lists ¥948 million capital and 11 members; the February 2013 establishment release listed ¥946 million and 10 initial legal entities, a dated formation-to-current change

equipment.detail: CEMS predicts factory energy demand, captures electricity/steam demand and tariff data, and calculates optimal cogeneration output versus utility purchases. The public case does not name a specific Yokogawa product model.

Reused nickel-metal-hydride batteries from Prius vehicles; current aggregate power is 90 kW, but chemistry detail, energy capacity, pack count, and current converter models were not disclosed in the reviewed current sheet.

A 7.8 MW city-gas engine cogeneration plant supplies electricity and recovered steam/hot water. Manufacturer, engine model, thermal rating, and heat-to-power ratio were not found in the reviewed public sources.

technicalDetails.detail: The CEMS coordinates utility electricity, gas cogeneration, solar generation, shared private/self-operated lines, and factory energy-management systems. It also dispatches recovered steam to the vehicle plant and hot water to the greenhouse.

Yokogawa's system forecasts each factory's demand, develops cogeneration and purchase schedules, and created a framework for member factories to participate voluntarily in energy sharing and future demand-response coordination.

Yokogawa reports that the April 2013-March 2015 demonstration reduced costs by 20% compared with the prior baseline. This is a supplier case-study result; no independent measurement protocol was found.
Toyota's community account says recovered heat supports year-round paprika production and reduced the greenhouse's fuel spending to roughly half that of the operator's comparable farms.
The LLP was established February 28, 2013; energy operations began in April 2013; the Yokogawa CEMS completed a two-year field demonstration and was declared fully operational in July 2015.
The project is in Ohira Village, Kurokawa District, Miyagi Prefecture, in the Second North Sendai Central Industrial Area—not Niiza, Saitama.

Toyota's current business sheet and December 2024 personnel profile support continued organizational activity, but no recent annual production, renewable fraction, CHP efficiency, emissions reduction, outage performance, or battery state-of-health data were located.

faial.summary: Faial's isolated Azores grid is a wind-diesel-hydro-solar system operated by EDA. Its best-documented microgrid intervention is ABB Powercorp's RMC 600 control system, commissioned in 2013 to coordinate the island's fuel-oil generation and the 4.25 MW Salão wind farm. Current EDA statistics show that the island remains predominantly thermal: 2023 grid emission totaled 48,384 MWh, including 42,810 MWh from fuel-oil and diesel generation, 5,369 MWh wind, 133 MWh hydro, and 72 MWh photovoltaic sources.
faial.status: The Faial island grid and its wind, thermal, hydro, and photovoltaic resources remain operational. The reviewed current EDA data do not explicitly confirm whether the original ABB RMC 600 controller is still in service in 2026, so the controller's current lifecycle status is unknown.

organizations.name: EDA - Electricidade dos Açores, S.A.
organizations.role: Island electric utility, grid operator, and operator of Faial's thermal generation and distribution system

organizations.name: EDA Renováveis, S.A.
organizations.role: Renewable-generation company responsible for the Salão wind farm and Varadouro hydro assets within the EDA group

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Supplier and commissioning organization for the RMC 600 renewable microgrid controller after acquiring Powercorp

organizations.role: Original developer of the RMC technology later acquired and delivered under ABB

organizations.name: Vestas
organizations.role: Supplier of the five V52 wind turbines at Salão

specifications.label: Salão wind farm
specifications.value: 4.25 MW: five Vestas V52 turbines rated 850 kW each, connected in 2013

specifications.label: Varadouro hydro plant
specifications.value: 0.320 MW; EDA Renováveis forecast 218 MWh for 2024 after an estimated 190 MWh in 2023

specifications.label: 2023 grid emission by source
specifications.value: 42,787 MWh thermal fuel oil, 23 MWh thermal diesel, 5,369 MWh wind, 133 MWh hydro, and 72 MWh photovoltaic; total 48,384 MWh

specifications.label: 2023 demand and peak
specifications.value: 46,523 MWh consumption and 9,449 kW annual peak

specifications.label: Distribution voltage
specifications.value: 15 kV medium-voltage island network

specifications.label: Electrical storage
specifications.value: No battery storage was documented as installed in the reviewed Faial control-system case; ABB material discussed PowerStore only as a possible future integration, not as an as-built asset

equipment.detail: Renewable microgrid controller commissioned in December 2013 to balance wind, island demand, and dispatch of the conventional generator fleet
Five 850 kW turbines at the Salão wind farm
EDA identifies heavy fuel oil as Faial's principal thermal fuel. Public sources reviewed here do not provide a current generator manufacturer/model inventory.

technicalDetails.detail: The RMC 600 calculated economical operating combinations, balanced generation and demand, maximized wind use subject to system limits, and coordinated loading, spinning reserve, and operating constraints for the conventional plant.
ABB announced the RMC 600 as delivered and commissioned on December 4, 2013, coinciding with integration of the expanded Salão wind resource.

ABB's infographic claimed fuel savings above 3.5 million liters per year. The reviewed source does not disclose the measurement baseline or independent verification, so this is retained as an ABB-reported benefit rather than a verified operating result.
EDA's 2023 figures show wind as the largest renewable source but thermal fuel oil still dominating annual production. Faial is therefore an operating hybrid island grid, not a renewable-only system.

The EU Clean Energy for EU Islands page identifies ongoing study of additional renewables and ferry electrification, but does not document a commissioned large battery or replacement controller for Faial.

Current RMC 600 service status, controller software version, present thermal-generator makes/models, installed battery capacity, and independently measured fuel savings were not found in public sources through July 2026.

fairfield-ct-microgrid.summary: Fairfield's public-safety microgrid links the town's police headquarters/emergency communications center, fire headquarters, Operation Hope shelter, and a cell site. Installed in 2015, it combines a 300 kW natural-gas standby generator, a 60 kW natural-gas CHP unit, and 47 kW of solar with Schneider Electric controls so the facilities can separate from the United Illuminating grid during an outage.
fairfield-ct-microgrid.status: Operational municipal microgrid, installed in 2015 and still cited as an operating regional resilience case in 2025. No public post-commissioning outage log or independently measured availability record was found through July 2026.

specifications.label: Critical-load margin
specifications.value: Regional and trade case studies describe available generation as approximately 120% of the connected critical peak, providing operating margin rather than a new nameplate rating

equipment.detail: Controller and islanding package linking public-safety loads and coordinating utility, solar, CHP, and standby generation. The reviewed source does not identify a controller product model.
60 kW natural-gas reciprocating engine with recovered heat; manufacturer and model were not disclosed in the reviewed public sources.
300 kW natural-gas unit serving the public-safety cluster; manufacturer and model were not disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: New electrical links connected the shelter, police/emergency communications facility, and fire headquarters. Controls permit grid-connected operation and intentional islanding while CHP heat is recovered for useful building service.
The islanded system supports police, fire, emergency communications, an emergency shelter, and a cellular communications site.
Connecticut awarded pilot funding in the first state microgrid round, and regional planning records identify the public-safety system as installed in 2015.

A 2025 Southwest Connecticut climate plan continues to use the project as an installed microgrid case, and Sustainable Fairfield separately lists completed microgrids at the Police Station and wastewater plant.

This record is the police/fire/shelter public-safety microgrid. Fairfield also developed a distinct wastewater-treatment-plant microgrid; capacities and equipment from that project should not be merged into this one.

No reviewed source publishes controller model, generator makes/models, CHP thermal output, measured fuel use, emissions, islanding-event history, reliability, or battery capacity; no battery was identified.

falkland-islands-microgrid.summary: The catalog record refers most accurately to Stanley's isolated electricity system, not one unified Falkland Islands grid. Stanley combines the 1.98 MW Sand Bay wind farm and three flywheel systems with legacy W.H. Allen diesels and three 2 MW Cummins interim sets. The existing hybrid system remains operational while the Falkland Islands Government develops Sand Bay Phase 3, a separate new Stanley power station, storage, and grid upgrades.
falkland-islands-microgrid.status: Existing Stanley wind-diesel-flywheel system operational. Sand Bay Phase 3 is procured/under development but not commissioned: three Enercon E70 turbines and an ABB grid-forming battery package were approved in 2024, and June 2026 reporting says the construction phase was deferred to January 2027 for safer weather conditions. The new Stanley power station is separately targeted for 2028.

organizations.name: Falkland Islands Government Public Works Department, Power & Electrical Section
organizations.role: Owner/operator of Stanley's power station, distribution network, and Sand Bay wind farm; project lead for Phase 3 and the replacement station

organizations.name: Enercon
organizations.role: Supplier of the existing E-33 turbines and contracted supplier of E70 turbines for Sand Bay Phase 3

organizations.name: W.H. Allen
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the legacy diesel engine fleet at Stanley B Power Station

organizations.name: Cummins
organizations.role: Supplier of three 2 MW high-speed interim generator sets commissioned in 2020

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Selected supplier of the Sand Bay Phase 3 battery energy-storage and power-conversion system

organizations.name: EPC Power
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the CAB1000 power-conversion platform specified within ABB's proposed Phase 3 storage solution

organizations.name: Innoven
organizations.role: Selected integration specialist for the Phase 3 power-management system and communications between Enercon and ABB equipment

organizations.name: British Forces South Atlantic Islands and UK Ministry of Defence
organizations.role: Partner and funder for the separate Mare Harbour Phase 2 project procured in parallel with Sand Bay Phase 3; Mare Harbour serves Mount Pleasant Complex, not the Stanley grid

specifications.label: Existing Sand Bay wind capacity
specifications.value: 1.98 MW using the Public Works rating: six Enercon E-33 turbines at 330 kW each. A 2024 Executive Council paper restated the same six machines as 335 kW each; this appears to be a documentation-rating discrepancy, not additional turbines.

specifications.label: Existing flywheel storage
specifications.value: Three flywheel systems commissioned with Sand Bay Phase 2 in February 2010; public current sources do not state power or energy ratings

specifications.label: Legacy diesel fleet
specifications.value: Eight W.H. Allen engines ranging from 320 kW to 1.5 MW at Stanley B Power Station

specifications.label: Interim diesel fleet
specifications.value: Three Cummins high-speed generator sets at 2 MW each, online since April 2020

specifications.label: Existing station and demand context
specifications.value: Energy Strategy reports 6.6 MW maximum existing station output and Stanley annual demand above 17 GWh

specifications.label: Existing renewable contribution
specifications.value: Public Works says Sand Bay supplies just over 30% of Stanley demand on average; another current government overview rounds the share to 40%, so the operator's more qualified figure is retained

specifications.label: Sand Bay Phase 3 wind
specifications.value: Three Enercon E70 turbines rated 2.3 MW each (6.9 MW total) approved/contracted, not yet commissioned

specifications.label: Sand Bay Phase 3 battery
specifications.value: An early November 2023 design described 8 MWh; the March 2024 procurement comparison describes a 10 MWh ABB package. Final commissioned rating is not yet available because construction is incomplete.

specifications.label: Planned new power station
specifications.value: Five-engine design: four 2.4 MW units plus one 1.6 MW unit, generating at 11 kV; completion targeted for 2028 and battery accommodation considered

specifications.label: Sand Bay solar trial
specifications.value: 12 kW trial array split between rooftop and ground-mounted configurations, with multi-year data collection planned before grid-scale expansion

equipment.detail: Six direct-drive units installed in two three-turbine phases in 2007 and 2010
Eight aging Allen units plus three 2 MW Cummins high-speed interim sets; individual Allen models and Cummins set models were not disclosed on the current Public Works page.
Three 2.3 MW Wind Class 1, direct-drive, full-converter turbines procured before the model's production ended; installation remains pending.

equipment.model: eStorage Flex with EPC Power CAB1000
equipment.detail: Approved integrated battery and modular 1 MW-block power-conversion platform supporting grid-forming, grid-following, black-start, active-power, and reactive-power modes; not yet commissioned.
Power-management integration intended to replace an aging Windows XP-based control system and coordinate Enercon converters with the ABB battery system.

technicalDetails.detail: Stanley uses wind and flywheels to reduce diesel dispatch on an isolated network. The flywheels damp short lags between load changes and generator response; they are fast-response stabilizers, not documented long-duration energy storage.

The current station generates at 3.3 kV and uses four step-up transformers for 11 kV distribution. The network reaches Stanley Airport, Mount Kent, Pony's Pass Quarry, and the abattoir and includes 96 substations; the new station is planned to generate directly at 11 kV.

The ABB battery's grid-forming inverter is intended to establish voltage and frequency, black-start the island system, and emulate diesel-generator behavior while Enercon turbines operate as grid-following converters. Modeling projected renewable utilization rising from 38% to as much as 81% and captured wind penetration from 21% to as much as 86%; these are forecasts, not operating results.

Executive Council approved the full joint renewable project and sole-source Enercon, ABB, and Innoven elements in March 2024. A February 2025 delivery update said works had commenced with estimated completion in Q2 FY2026/27. June 2026 reporting states that construction at Sand Bay and Mare Harbour was deferred to January 2027 for safer seasonal conditions.

Government planning documents expect Sand Bay Phase 1 turbines to reach planned end of life in 2027 and Phase 2 in 2030. Phase 3 is intended both to raise renewable supply and avoid a return to complete diesel reliance as those units retire.

Stanley/Sand Bay is separate from the Mare Harbour wind farm serving the British military's Mount Pleasant Complex and from small Camp settlement systems. Capacity procured for Mare Harbour must not be added to Stanley's microgrid.

The reviewed public record does not disclose the three existing flywheels' maker, model, power, or energy capacity; final as-delivered Phase 3 battery rating; new power-station engine manufacturer/models; or a recent audited annual wind/diesel generation table.
